techniques.evidence_quote: Consequently, whenever bignum was downloaded or re-installed, the users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file, placed by the attacker.

When the bucket is deleted, the name becomes available again... an unidentified attacker noticed the sudden abandonment of a once-active AWS bucket. Recognizing an opportunity, the attacker seized the abandoned bucket.

techniques.technique_name: Match Legitimate Resource Name or Location
techniques.evidence_quote: This counterfeit .node binary mimicked the functions of the original file. It carried out the usual and expected activities of the package.

Starting with an endless list of byte additions to registries, comparisons, and data movements that initially seemed pointless... a URL was constructed by individually reversing the string parts. Scanning the file using virus total did not yield any results.

the binary file harvested data via functions like getpwd and getuid, extracting environmental data... Malicious binaries steal the user id's, passwords, local machine environment variables, and local host name

techniques.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
techniques.evidence_quote: It then created a TCP socket for IPv4 communication and covertly sent the collected data as a user-agent of a 'GET' request... exfiltrates the stolen data to the hijacked bucket.

attack_chain.description: The attacker identified that the S3 bucket previously used by npm package 'bignum' (versions 0.12.2–0.13.0) to host pre-built binaries had been deleted, making its globally unique name available again. The attacker re-registered the abandoned S3 bucket, effectively hijacking it.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: When the bucket is deleted, the name becomes available again... an unidentified attacker noticed the sudden abandonment of a once-active AWS bucket. Recognizing an opportunity, the attacker seized the abandoned bucket.

attack_chain.description: The attacker placed a malicious pre-compiled .node binary (C/C++) in the hijacked S3 bucket, replacing the legitimate binary that 'bignum' package versions downloaded at install time via node-gyp. Any developer installing the affected bignum versions would automatically download the trojanized binary.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Consequently, whenever bignum was downloaded or re-installed, the users unknowingly downloaded the malicious binary file, placed by the attacker.

attack_chain.technique_name: Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name or Location
attack_chain.description: The malicious binary faithfully reproduced the expected legitimate behavior of the original bignum binary so users would not notice anything suspicious during or after installation.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: This counterfeit .node binary mimicked the functions of the original file. It carried out the usual and expected activities of the package.

attack_chain.description: The malicious payload within the binary was heavily obfuscated using byte-level register operations and reversed string construction to conceal its C2 URL, evading antivirus detection (VirusTotal returned no results).
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Starting with an endless list of byte additions to registries, comparisons, and data movements that initially seemed pointless... a URL was constructed by individually reversing the string parts. Scanning the file using virus total did not yield any results.

attack_chain.description: Upon execution, the malicious binary used OS-level functions (getpwd, getuid) to harvest the victim's user ID, password, local environment variables, and hostname.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: the binary file harvested data via functions like getpwd and getuid, extracting environmental data... Malicious binaries steal the user id's, passwords, local machine environment variables, and local host name

attack_chain.technique_name: Exfiltration Over Web Service: Exfiltration to Cloud Storage
attack_chain.description: The binary created a TCP socket and exfiltrated the stolen credentials and system data to the attacker-controlled hijacked S3 bucket by embedding the data covertly in the User-Agent header of an HTTP GET request.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It then created a TCP socket for IPv4 communication and covertly sent the collected data as a user-agent of a 'GET' request... exfiltrates the stolen data to the hijacked bucket.

initial_access_vector: Attacker hijacked an abandoned/deleted S3 bucket previously used by npm package 'bignum' to host pre-built binaries, then replaced the legitimate binary with a malicious one; any developer installing affected bignum versions (0.12.2–0.13.0) automatically downloaded the trojanized binary.
persistence_mechanism: The hijacked S3 bucket served as persistent malicious infrastructure — as long as the bucket remained under attacker control, every fresh installation or reinstallation of affected bignum versions would deliver the malicious binary.
privilege_escalation: None observed; the binary executed with the privileges of the installing user process.
